coredata.dc:title: Enhancing photoluminescence stability of blue emissive nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots by encapsulating inside zeolitic imidazole framework-8
coredata.dc:creator: Balakrishnan, Thivyah
Ang, Wei Lun
Mahmoudi, Ebrahim
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as promising materials for luminescence sensing in chemical and biological fields due to their excellent optical properties. However, their limited stability hinders their practical applications. To address this, we encapsulated the nitrogen doped GQDs (nGQDs) within zeolitic imidazole framework-8 (ZIF-8) via bottle-around-ship method, resulting in nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. This encapsulation process was conducted at room temperature and aimed to enhance the photo-, thermal, pH, and storage stability of nGQDs. Through nitrogen doping, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of GQDs was greatly enhanced by 97.3%. The nGQD@ZIF-8 retained 93.5% and 91% of its initial PL intensity after continuous exposure of 12 h UV light, and heated to 90 °C, respectively. Moreover, the PL intensity of nGQD@ZIF-8 remained relatively constant within a broad pH range (pH 2 to pH 11) and after 3 months of storage. The ZIF-8 encapsulation efficiently provides surface passivation, minimizing surface defects and enhancing the stability of nGQDs. It also effectively shields the nGQDs under harsh conditions through confinement and framework protection. The exceptional properties of the resulting nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite broaden its practical applicability in multifunctional fields such as bioimaging, chemical and biological sensing probes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as promising materials for luminescence sensing in chemical and biological fields due to their excellent optical properties. However, their limited stability hinders their practical applications. To address this, we encapsulated the nitrogen doped GQDs (nGQDs) within zeolitic imidazole framework-8 (ZIF-8) via bottle-around-ship method, resulting in nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. This encapsulation process was conducted at room temperature and aimed to enhance the photo-, thermal, pH, and storage stability of nGQDs. Through nitrogen doping, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of GQDs was greatly enhanced by 97.3%. The nGQD@ZIF-8 retained 93.5% and 91% of its initial PL intensity after continuous exposure of 12 h UV light, and heated to 90 °C, respectively. Moreover, the PL intensity of nGQD@ZIF-8 remained relatively constant within a broad pH range (pH 2 to pH 11) and after 3 months of storage. The ZIF-8 encapsulation efficiently provides surface passivation, minimizing surface defects and enhancing the stability of nGQDs. It also effectively shields the nGQDs under harsh conditions through confinement and framework protection. The exceptional properties of the resulting nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite broaden its practical applicability in multifunctional fields such as bioimaging, chemical and biological sensing probes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as a fascinating class of nanomaterials in the carbon family, exhibiting exceptional properties such as size-tunable photoluminescence (PL) [1], high electron mobility, and excellent biocompatibility [2]. These attributes have paved the way for their potential use in diverse fields, including chemical sensing [3,4], bioimaging [5], optoelectronics [6], energy storage, and catalysis. However, the inherent instability of GQDs, especially under ambient conditions, has impeded their widespread practical application. The stability issue of GQDs arises due to their highly reactive edges and oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface, which are susceptible to degradation upon exposure to moisture, oxygen, and light [7]. Consequently, the PL efficiency and electrical properties of GQDs deteriorate rapidly, limiting their long-term performance and reliability. Overcoming this stability challenge is crucial for unlocking the full potential of GQDs and realizing their practical applications.

To address the stability concerns of quantum dots (QDs), various strategies have been explored, including surface passivation with organic molecules and encapsulation within protective matrices. Utilizing a QD@MOF composite by merging QDs with metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) is a highly efficient approach for enhancing the stability of QDs [8]. MOFs are extensively employed due to their porosity, significant surface area, unoccupied metal sites, and diverse structural characteristics [9,10]. Their high stability and inherent high porosity allow for efficient access of guest molecules, leading to successful integration with quantum dots [11–13], metal nanoparticles [14–16], and dye molecules [17], resulting in the creation of novel multifunctional composites. The geometrical structure of MOFs can prevent aggregation and leaching of embedded QDs, thus improving its stability under harsh conditions [18]. For instance, Liu et al. synthesized highly fluorescent cadmium telluride QDs embedded Zirconium-MOFs (CdTe@ZrMOF) nanocomposite that maintained the same PL intensity after 80 days of storage, demonstrating good PL stability [19]. In another study, Zhang et al. reported that CH3NH3PbBr3 PQDs encapsulated within Bio-MOF-1 were able to retain their fluorescence after 90 days of exposure to air compared to bare CH3NH3PbBr3 PQDs that lost their fluorescence within 5 min of exposure, demonstrating a notable enhancement in air-stability [20]. Furthermore, Xu et al. successfully encapsulated carbon dots within zeolitic imidazole framework (ZIF-8), exhibiting high stability even at elevated temperatures with only about 25% weight loss at 400–700 °C and good stability against photobleaching with no obvious PL change when exposed to natural light for 1 month [21]. The impressive capabilities exhibited by QD@MOF nanocomposites make them promising candidates for both loading and protecting the unstable GQDs especially under ambient conditions. However, it is worth noting that while research papers often focus on the enhanced performance of GQDs in specific applications, they frequently overlook the equally crucial aspect of enhanced stability achieved through their encapsulation into MOFs. Ensuring enhanced stability of GQDs is essential for maintaining consistent performance, prolonging their lifespan, and improving overall reliability, thereby can unlock the full range of benefits offered by GQDs-based technologies.

Moreover, chemical doping of heteroatoms (nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), boron (B), phosphorus (P), etc.) into GQDs has been found to significantly enhance the luminescence properties of GQDs [22]. Heteroatoms doping is a key strategy to increase the radiative recombination rate of electron-hole pairs within the GQDs, resulting in a higher quantum yield. This enhancement leads to brighter and more intense luminescence from the doped GQDs. Recently, several research studies have demonstrated the successful synthesis of N-doped GQDs [23,24], B-doped GQDs [25], S-doped GQDs [26], and N/S/P/B- doped GQDs [27] with remarkable high quantum yields. For example, the N-doped GQDs exhibit a PL emission intensity at 443 nm that is approximately 25 times higher than that of undoped GQDs, demonstrated by Das et al. [24]. Similarly, Juang et al. successfully developed B-doped GQDs with adjustable optical properties by manipulating the concentration of B-dopant [25]. This manipulation resulted in remarkably high quantum yields of green and red fluorescence, reaching 99.8% and 17.1%, respectively.

In this work, we synthesized blue emissive N-doped GQDs (nGQDs) via direct pyrolysis and subsequently encapsulated them within ZIF-8 via a simple room-temperature method. Ethylenediamine (EDA) was chosen as the doping agent due to its strong electron donor properties, effectively passivating the surface defects of GQDs and reducing non-radiative recombination processes, resulting in enhanced PL intensity. The small molecular size of EDA ensured uniform doping, and its availability and compatibility made it a cost-effective choice. Apart from that, our selection of ZIF-8 as the encapsulating MOF was based on several considerations: Firstly, unique structural properties of ZIF-8, such as a zeolite-like structure composed of imidazolate linkers and metal ions, provide a robust and protective environment for guest species; Moreover, the tunable pore size of ZIF-8 allows for precise control over the encapsulated nGQDs' size, preventing aggregation and maintaining their dispersibility. The nGQDs, ZIF-8 and nGQD@ZIF-8 were characterized using different techniques (HRTEM, SEM, PL spectroscopy, UV–vis spectroscopy, FTIR, XRD, XPS, BET, Zeta potential and TGA). The molar ratio of acid to amine, the pyrolysis duration and reaction temperature were optimized to enhance PL intensity of nGQDs. Furthermore, the stability of nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposites under different conditions (thermal stability test, photostability test, storage stability test, and pH stability) was examined to assess effectiveness of the ZIF-8 encapsulation in improving stability of nGQDs.

Citric acid (CA) and triethylamine (TEA) were purchased from Fisher Chemical, UK and Friendemann Schmidt, Malaysia, respectively. EDA, 2-Methylimidazole (2-MeIM) and Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O) were supplied by Sigma Aldrich from France, India, and Germany, respectively. All chemicals were used without further purification.

The nGQDs were synthesized by a direct pyrolysis method with slight modification [28]. Firstly, 2 g of CA and 318, 636, 954 and 1273 μL of EDA (i.e., the molar ratio of the CA to the EDA was 1.0:0.5, 1.0:1.0, 1.0:1.5, and 1.0:2.0) were mixed in a 50 mL round-bottom flask and heated at temperatures of 175–225 °C for periods of 6–8 min. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solid product was dissolved by 100 mL deionized (DI) water and the resultant nGQDs solution was filtered using syringe filter (0.22 μm) to remove any large fragments. Finally, the brown solution containing nGQDs and stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

The nGQD@ZIF-8 composites were synthesized via bottle-around-ship strategy. The synthesis procedure is detailed as follows. Firstly, 2-MeIM (3.31 g) was dissolved in 48.45 g of DI water by stirring on a magnetic stirrer (at 200 rpm). Then, 3 mL of TEA was added while stirring. At the same time, 2 g of Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O was dissolved in 12.11 g of DI water under constant stirring at 200 rpm. Both the solutions and different amounts of nGQDs solution (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 and 30 mL) were mixed and then vigorously stirred at 500 rpm for 30 min at room temperature. The samples were correspondingly named as S-0, S-5, S-10, S-15, S-20, S-25 and S-30, respectively. Finally, the products were collected by centrifugation (8000 rpm for 15 min) and rinsed with DI water for three times to remove excess nGQDs before overnight drying (60 °C). The ZIF-8 was synthesized using above mentioned procedure without addition of nGQDs, which was referred from previously reported literature [29].

The fluorescence spectra were recorded with single beam spectrofluorophotometer (Edinburg Instrument, UK) at room temperature. The morphologies of the samples were observed using transmission electron microscope (TEM, Thermo Fisher- Talos 120C, USA) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) by Zeiss Merlin, Germany. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) were obtained using Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer; 2θ angle was scanned between 0° and 50°. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface areas and porosities were studied with a nitrogen adsorption instrument (Micromeritics Tristar II Plus, USA) after the samples were first degassed at 180 °C overnight. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) experiments were performed on a Shimadzu TGA 50 under nitrogen atmosphere at a rate of 10 °C/min, between 25 and 800 °C. To identify the surface functional groups of the synthesized samples, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, Germany) was used. UV–vis absorption was characterized by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer- Lamda 35, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were acquired with a Japan Kratos Axis UltraDLD spectrometer using a monochromatic Al Kɑ source at 1486.6 eV. ZETA Sizer (Malvern In- struments Pvt. Ltd., UK) was used to measure the surface charges of the samples. All pH measurements were made using digital pH-meter (827 PH LAB, Switzerland). UV viewing cabinet (Vilber Laurmat, France) was used as UV light source in this study.

The quantum yields of the samples were calculated using the following equation [30]:

where Φ and F are the results of the quantum yield and the PL emission intensity, respectively, and η and A are the refractive index and the absorbance at 365 nm, respectively. The subscripts “R" and “S" refer to the reference material and sample, respectively. In this case, a 5 μM quinine sulfate (ΦR = 0.54) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (η = 1.33) while the samples were dissolved in DI water (η = 1.33) as preparation for the measurements.
The visual images for each test were taken under 365 nm UV radiation using an iPhone 13 equipped with a 12-mega- pixel rear camera.

Photostability refers to the characteristics of a material that indicate its stability when exposed to prolonged UV radiation and open atmosphere. Prior to the experiment, 6 vials containing nGQDs solution (each 3 mL) and another 6 vials containing nGQD@ZIF-8 powder (each 15 mg) were prepared. In the photostability test, both the nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 samples were subjected to a) UV exposure using a 120 W UV lamp (365 nm) placed inside dark chamber, with the samples positioned at 15 cm from the lamp. b) exposure to an open atmosphere for a duration of 12 h. Every 2 h, a vial from each set of samples was retrieved to measure their PL intensities.

Thermal stability is a material property that indicates its stability under various thermal conditions. In this study, the thermal stability of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 was evaluated using three different approaches: a) Firstly, a vial containing 3 mL of nGQDs solution and another vial containing 15 mg of nGQD@ZIF-8 powder were immersed in a hot oil bath maintained at temperature range, specifically from 30 °C to 90 °C for 30 min. b) Secondly, a vial containing 3 mL of nGQDs solution and another vial containing 15 mg of nGQD@ZIF-8 powder were placed in hot water bath at a constant high temperature of 70 °C with an interval of 10 min for 60 min. c) Thirdly, 15 mg of nGQD@ZIF-8 powder dispersed in 10 mL of DI water was subjected to heating test at temperature of 70 °C for 24 h. The uniform exposure of the samples to the heat was ensured in each test. After each heating test, the samples were cooled to room temperature and the PL intensity of the samples was then measured upon excitation at 365 nm to assess their thermal stability.

In the storage stability test, 10 mL of nGQDs solution and 0.5 g of nGQD@ZIF-8 powder were stored at room temperature for a period of 3 months. Throughout the duration of the test, the PL intensity of the samples was measured twice a month to monitor any changes.

The pH stability of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 was evaluated by measuring the change in PL intensity under various pH conditions ranging from pH 2 to pH 11. A series of solutions with different pH values (2, 5, 7, 9, and 11) were prepared. Subsequently, 3 mL of pH 2 solution was added into a vial containing 3 mL of nGQDs solution. Simultaneously, another vial containing 15 mg of nGQD@ZIF-8 powder was immersed with 3 mL of pH 2 solution. Both mixtures were left to react for 30 min before measuring their PL intensities. The same procedure was carried out for the remaining pH solutions (5, 7, 9 and 11).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and composition of nGQDs, ZIF-8 and nGQD@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1 a, CA is used as carbon source and EDA as N dopant to synthesis blue emissive nGQDs solution by direct pyrolisis method. The formation of nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposites was achieved using the bottle-around-ship technique, as presented in Scheme 1b. In this process, pre-synthesized nGQDs were added to precursor solutions of ZIF-8 and allowed to react under conditions similar to those required for producing pure ZIF-8. The morphology of nGQDs, ZIF-8, and nGQD@ZIF-8 was examined using TEM and SEM imaging techniques. TEM images of well-dispersed spherical nGQDs are shown in Fig. 1 a. The size distribution of the synthesized nGQDs ranged from 2.25 to 5.75 nm, with an average size of 3.85 nm, as depicted in Fig. 1b. The ZIF-8 nanoparticles exhibited a rhombic dodecahedron morphology with an average diameter of 200 nm (Fig. 1c), consistent with existing literature [31,32]. Moreover, the external morphology of nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposites displayed a rhombic dodecahedron structure similar to ZIF-8 (Fig. 1d), with an average size of approximately 160 nm. Furthermore, SEM images in Fig. 1e and f clearly demonstrate the respective materials, revealing a nearly identical hexagonal shape in both ZIF-8 and nGQD@ZIF-8. This similarity in size and shape indicates that the addition of nGQDs does not alter the overall morphology of pure ZIF-8. The elemental composition of nGQDs, ZIF-8, and nGQD@ZIF-8 was studied using EDX analysis. The EDX spectra shows the presence of C, O, and N in nGQDs (Fig. A1a) and Zn, C, O, and N in ZIF-8 (Fig. A1b). The observed spectrum of nGQD@ZIF-8 (Fig. A1c) showed strong peaks for C, O, N and Zn, demonstrating the successful formation of the nanocomposite. The data about the weight percentages obtained for C, N, O, and Zn is given in the inset of the spectrums.

In order to confirm the successful formation of nGQD@ZIF-8, the samples were measured by XRD and FTIR. Both ZIF-8 and nGQD@ZIF-8 display a reflection at 2θ = 7.3°, corresponding to a 110 peak (Fig. 2 ), which indicates that both the nanocrystals consist of rhombic dodecahedron shape with 12 facets [33] and aligned well with the observed TEM images. When compared to the simulated ZIF-8 pattern, distinct diffraction peaks assigned to pure ZIF-8 can be observed in both the as-prepared ZIF-8 crystals and nGQD@ZIF-8 composites. This observation provides strong evidence that the introduction of nGQDs does not compromise the crystalline integrity of ZIF-8. The preservation of ZIF-8's structural integrity is likely due to the low concentrations and small sizes of the hosted nGQDs within the ZIF-8 matrix [21]. Furthermore, the diffraction peaks of nGQDs@ZIF-8 are narrower, indicating that the incorporation of nGQDs into ZIF-8 is the cause of this alteration [34]. The XRD patterns of nGQD@ZIF-8 display a broad peak within the 20–30 (2θ) range, which corresponds to the diffraction peak observed for nGQDs.

Fig. 3 a depicts the Raman spectra patterns of nGQDs, ZIF-8 and the nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite. The nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite exhibits characteristic peaks corresponding to both nGQDs and ZIF-8. In the Raman spectrum, the distinctive nGQDs peaks include the D band (1342 cm−1) and the G band (1583 cm−1), where the D band arises from local defects and disorders, especially those located at the edges of graphene and graphite platelets, and the G band originates from sp2 bonds of carbon atoms [35]. However, the peak centers of nGQDs narrow and shift to 1348 and 1588 cm−1 after encapsulation within ZIF-8, indicating a potential strong interaction between nGQDs and ZIF-8 [36]. Furthermore, a lower ID/IG ratio (0.82) for the nGQD@ZIF-8 than the value obtained for pristine nGQDs (ID/IG = 0.87) could be due to the interactions between the surface of nGQDs and the surface of ZIF-8 matrix [37]. These values also signify the improved quality (comparatively less defects) of nGQDs, resulting from the stronger confinement within the ZIF-8 crystals. Simultaneously, the obtained Raman peaks of ZIF-8 align with the previous reports with strong peaks at 691 cm−1, 1145 cm−1, and 1454 cm−1 correspond to the bending vibration of the imidazole ring, C–N stretching vibration, and methyl bending vibration, respectively. The stretching vibration of the C–H in the methyl group occurs at 2923 cm−1, and the C–H stretching vibration on the imidazole ring emerges at 3124 cm−1. Moreover, the peaks of nGQD@ZIF-8 match well with the pristine ZIF-8 with no observable peak shifts, indicating that no obvious structural defects in ZIF-8 after nGQDs encapsulation, which is consistent with the XRD and TEM data.

Fig. A2 displays the FTIR spectra of nGQDs, ZIF-8, and nGQD@ZIF-8. The infrared characteristic peaks corresponding to the N–H and C–N bonds of nGQDs, indicate successful EDA doping, resulting in abundant amino groups on the surface of nGQDs, providing recognition sites [38]. Furthermore, in nGQD@ZIF-8, the presence of an infrared peak at 1628 cm−1 and 3520 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibrations of CC and O–H, originating from nGQDs [31]. Comparing the FT-IR spectra of nGQDs and ZIF-8, it is evident that the nGQD@ZIF-8 composite exhibits distinct infrared peaks characteristic of both nGQDs and ZIF-8. In addition, Fig. 3b reveals that both the nGQD@ZIF-8 dispersion and nGQD@ZIF-8 powder (S) exhibit an absorption shoulder at 242 nm and a prominent absorption peak around 365 nm in the UV–vis absorption range. These absorptions are attributed to nGQDs and align with the optimal PL excitation peak at 365 nm. The peak at 365 nm is associated with the n-π* transition of CO bonds, while the shoulder at 242 nm is identified as the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds [39]. These observations further confirm the successful encapsulation of nGQDs into the matrix of ZIF-8.

XPS analysis was performed to examine the surface chemical composition and oxidation state of ZIF-8 and the nGQD@ZIF-8 composite. The binding energies were calibrated using the C 1s peak at 284.6 eV as reference. The survey spectra (Fig. 4 a) of both ZIF-8 and nGQD@ZIF-8 revealed prominent peaks corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Zn 2p. High-resolution XPS spectra for C 1s (Fig. 4b) exhibited three peaks for ZIF-8 at 284.8, 285.2, and 286.0 eV, representing CC, C–N, and C–OH, respectively. In contrast, the C 1s spectrum for nGQD@ZIF-8 displayed four peaks at 284.8, 285.2, 286.1, and 287.3 eV, corresponding to CC, C–N, C–OH and CO, respectively. The extra carbon peak (CO) in nGQD@ZIF-8 indicated the nGQDs were well embedded into ZIF-8 matrix. The C1s spectrum of nGQDs have also been presented for comparison, which show three carbon peaks (CC, C–OH and CO) with binding energy values 283.9, 285.9 and 287.6 eV respectively (Fig. A. 3a). Similar observations have been reported for C1s by previous studies [31,37]. The N 1s spectra (Fig. 4c) showed two peaks for ZIF-8 at 399.9 and 400.6 eV, assigned to C–N and CN in 2-methylimidazole [40]. The two peaks of N 1s for nGQD@ZIF-8 moved slightly towards the low binding energy and were located at 398.8 and 399.7 eV, respectively, demonstrating an interaction between nGQDs and ZIF-8 [31]. The O 1s spectra (Fig. 4d) for ZIF-8 were deconvoluted into two peaks corresponding to O–Zn, and O–C at binding energies of 531.2, and 532.1 eV, respectively. The O 1s peaks for nGQD@ZIF-8 slightly shifted towards higher binding energies, located at 530.7, and 532.2 eV, indicating an interaction between nGQDs and ZIF-8. The Zn 2p spectra (Fig. 4e) for ZIF-8 displayed a distinct doublet at 1021.2 and 1044.1 eV, assigned to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 of Zn2+. In comparison, the Zn 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 peaks of nGQD@ZIF-8 shifted towards lower binding energies, positioned at 1020.5 and 1043.6 eV, respectively. This shift indicated a reduction in the oxidation state of Zn due to nGQDs modification, leading to an increase in electron density on Zn [31,40].

Fig. 5 illustrates the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of the samples. Both ZIF-8 and nGQD@ZIF-8 exhibit type IV isotherms, indicating that they are multi-stage pore materials with a combination of micropores and mesopores (Fig. 5a and b), in accordance with the IUPAC classification [41]. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of ZIF-8 and nGQD@ZIF-8 share the same type, suggesting that the structure of ZIF-8 remains intact after the incorporation of nGQDs. This finding is consistent with the XRD data. The BET surface area of nGQD@ZIF-8 (526.23 m2 g−1) is lower than that of pure ZIF-8 (581.18 m2 g−1), indicating a decrease in surface area due to the incorporation of nonporous nGQDs with loading amount of 9.42 wt% into ZIF-8. Furthermore, while the pore sizes of pure ZIF-8 are primarily distributed at 2.7 nm, the pore sizes of nGQD@ZIF-8 are centered at 3.8 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 5c. This shift in pore size distribution towards larger sizes suggests that the addition of nGQDs influences the pore characteristics of ZIF-8 [42]. Additionally, the encapsulation process of nGQDs within the ZIF-8 matrix can be confirmed through Zeta-potential analysis. As depicted in Fig. 6 , the surface potential of nGQDs exhibits a negative charge, while the surface potential of ZIF-8 shows a positive charge. According to theoretical expectations, when negatively charged nGQDs (−3.60 mV) are encapsulated within the highly positive charged ZIF-8 matrix (6.28 mV), the positive surface charge of formed nanocomposite should increase [43]. Notably, the surface potential of nGQD@ZIF-8 is observed to be positively charged (2.64 mV), aligning with the theoretical predictions. This outcome further validates strong electrostatic interactions, leading to successful encapsulation of nGQDs within the ZIF-8 matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL properties of nGQDs, ZIF-8 and nGQD@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of fluorescent nGQDs is greatly affected by synthesis conditions including the molar ratio between CA and EDA, the reaction temperature, and pyrolysis duration. Thus, to achieve good PL properties of the synthesized nGQDs, the above synthesis conditions were investigated, and the measured quantum yields, and the obtained PL spectra (optimal excitation wavelength of 365 nm) are shown in Fig. 7 a–c and Fig. A. 4a-c, respectively. Fig. 7a shows the quantum yield of nGQDs obtained under different molar ratio of CA and EDA at fixed reaction temperature of 200 °C and pyrolysis time of 7 min. The PL quantum yield of nGQDs increased gradually with increasing the molar ratio of CA to EDA from 1.0:0.0 to 1.0:1.0 and reached the highest quantum yield of 34.45% at the molar ratio of 1.0:1.0. However, a further increase in the molar ratio to 1.0:2.0 caused a decline in the quantum yield, which might be due to excessive nitrogen resulting in surface defects of nGQDs [39]. These defects act as trapping sites for excited electrons, which lead to PL quenching. Therefore, the best molar ratio of CA to EDA was set at 1.0:1.0. The origin of the PL enhancement (97.3%) in nGQDs can be explained by analyzing the N configurations. As illustrated in Fig. A. 3b, the addition of EDA as a dopant leads to a decrease in the density of pyrrolic N while increasing the density of pyridinic N and graphitic N. The decreased density of pyrrolic N in nGQDs results in a reduction of non-radiative recombination processes, thereby enhancing the radiative recombination rate and leading to an increased PL quantum yield. Conversely, the increased density of pyridinic N and graphitic N in nGQDs enhances the radiative recombination rate, consequently resulting in a higher PL intensity [23].

Furthermore, the effects of reaction temperature and pyrolysis duration on the PL quantum yield of nGQDs (CA to EDA ratio of 1.0:1.0) were investigated. Fig. 7b clearly illustrates that the PL quantum yield gradually increased up to a maximum value of 33.91% as the temperature increased to 200 °C, while maintaining a constant pyrolysis duration of 7 min. However, further increases in temperature resulted in a subsequent decrease in PL quantum yield. Regarding pyrolysis duration, the nGQDs synthesized with a reaction time of 7 min at a constant temperature of 200 °C exhibited the highest PL quantum yield of 33.73% (Fig. 7c) among the samples synthesized with different durations. Samples subjected to higher temperatures (>200 °C) and longer durations (>7 min) underwent over carbonization, leading to a decline in PL intensity [44]. This decline can be attributed to changes in the carbon-to-oxygen ratio, indicating oxidation or introduction of oxygen-related functional groups on the surface of nGQDs [45]. The lower carbon-to-oxygen ratio observed in these samples (Table A. 1) contributes to the loss of PL properties, indicating a more oxidized surface or the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups such as carbonyl (CO) or carboxyl (COOH) groups. These oxygen-containing groups can introduce energy dissipation pathways, such as non-radiative recombination or electron transfer processes, which ultimately result in a decrease in PL intensity [46]. Therefore, a pyrolysis temperature of 200 °C and a reaction duration of 7 min were determined as the best conditions for synthesizing fluorescent nGQDs with high PL quantum yield for all subsequent studies reported in this work.

Moreover, the nGQD@ZIF-8 dispersion, when observed under natural light, appears milky white and demonstrates strong blue fluorescence upon exposure to 365 nm ultraviolet light (Fig. A. 5). In Fig. 8 a, the PL emission occurs at 465 nm when excited at 365 nm, similar to that of nGQDs. From Fig. A. 4d, it can be observed that the pure ZIF-8 (S-0) does not exhibit a distinct peak at 465 nm, indicating the blue fluorescence observed in nGQD@ZIF-8 originates from the nGQDs rather than the ZIF-8 crystals. The PL intensity and quantum yield of nGQD@ZIF-8 could be regulated by varying the loading amount of the nGQDs, as shown in Fig. A. 4d and Fig. 7d. The quantities of nGQDs in ZIF-8 were determined by mixing varying volumes of nGQDs solution (0.1 g mL−1), namely 0 mL (S-0), 5 mL (S-5), 10 mL (S-10), 15 mL (S-15), 20 mL (S-20), 25 mL (S-25), and 30 mL (S-30). The calculated loading amounts for each nGQDs solution were found to be 1.88 wt%, 3.76 wt%, 5.65 wt%, 7.53 wt%, 9.42 wt%, and 11.29 wt%. These calculations assumed the conversion of all zinc atoms to ZIF-8, with a total mass of 5.31 g. It was observed that increasing the loading amount of nGQDs from 1.88 wt% to 7.53 wt% resulted in a significant 77.09% increase in the PL quantum yield. However, further increasing the loading amount to 9.42 wt% resulted in an almost similar quantum yield to that of 7.53 wt%. At 11.29 wt% of nGQDs, the PL quantum yield significantly decreased due to concentration quenching at higher loading. Concentration-induced PL quenching is a well-known phenomenon that occurs when the amount of QDs exceeds a critical concentration in the solvent or solid matrices [47]. Therefore, a loading amount of 7.53 wt% of nGQDs (S-20) has been determined as the best condition to prepare highly blue emissive nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposites in this study. The PL lifetime of nGQD@ZIF-8 was as long as 14.3 ns (Fig. 8b). The longer lifetime of the nanocomposite than that of pure nGQDs may be attributed to the surface passivation, which might reduce nonradiative relaxation from surface traps of the encapsulated nGQDs [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL stability of nGQDs, and nGQD@ZIF-8

ce:section.ce:para: The photostability test is crucial for assessing the durability and long-term performance of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8, particularly in applications involving long term exposure to light sources (UV radiation) such as bioimaging. For instance, the improved photostability enhance the bioimaging quality by maintaining consistent fluorescence emission over time, resulting in clearer insights into cellular structures and functions [49]. The nGQD@ZIF-8 retained 93.5% of its initial PL intensity upon UV radiation for 12 h (Fig. 9 a). In Fig. 9b, minimal attenuation was observed after 12 h of continuous exposure of nGQD@ZIF-8 to open atmosphere (average temperature of 25 °C and average humidity of 18%). However, major attenuations were observed in nGQDs under both continuous UV illumination and open atmosphere for 12 h, with only 38% and 60% of its original PL intensity maintained, respectively, accompanying with visual change from bright to faded blue (Fig. A. 6). These observations were further verified by calculating the rate of photodegradation by analyzing the changes in the absorption spectra of both nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 upon 12 h of continuous UV radiation. The nGQD@ZIF-8 exhibited a significantly low rate of photodegradation (5.3%) compared to bare nGQDs (37.6%), highlighting the remarkable photobleaching resistance of nGQD@ZIF-8. Additionally, the nanocomposite structure remained stable even after prolonged exposure to UV radiation and open atmosphere, as evidenced by the no observable changes in the XRD spectra (Fig. A. 7a) before and after such exposure. These results indicate that the ZIF-8 serves as a protective barrier around nGQDs, preventing high energy UV photons from breaking the chemical bonds of functional groups on the nGQDs and preserve their original optical properties [50].

The TGA was employed to characterize the thermostability of ZIF-8, nGQDs, and nGQD@ZIF-8, as illustrated in Fig. 10 a. The first weight loss of nGQDs around 120 °C can be attributed to the loss of adsorbed water molecules. The subsequent decrease in weight observed around 225 °C corresponds to the removal of oxygen containing surface functional groups [51]. The final weight loss of nGQDs occurred at 320 °C which is ascribed to the breakdown of carbon backbone. However, the TGA curve of ZIF-8 shows clear degradation temperatures at 270 °C and 400 °C, suggesting a higher degree of thermal stability in comparison to nGQDs. This difference in stability can be attributed to nGQDs containing relatively less stable oxygen groups, such as hydroxyl groups. In the case of nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite, the stability is maintained up to 255 °C. This is evident from the fact that the percentage of weight loss for nGQD@ZIF-8 (27%) is lower compared to that of nGQDs (18%) and similar to that of ZIF-8 when subjected to a temperature of 600 °C. These results suggest that nGQDs are successfully integrated into ZIF-8 while maintaining their original exceptional properties and the enhanced thermal stability of nGQD@ZIF-8 is a result of the interactions between the ZIF-8 and nGQDs. Furthermore, the thermal stability of nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 was evaluated using two methods. First, the PL intensity of nGQDs exhibited a significant reduction as the temperature increased from 20 °C to 90 °C, as depicted in Fig. 10b. Conversely, the PL intensity of nGQD@ZIF-8 remained unchanged until 40 °C and then decreased by 9% of its maximum value as the temperature reached 90 °C. Secondly, the nGQD@ZIF-8 samples were subjected to a constant high temperature (70 °C) for 60 min in an aqueous solution. Notably, no loss of luminescence was observed during the initial 20 min, but the PL intensity decreased by 6% of its initial value over the course of 60 min, as demonstrated in Fig. 10c. By further extending the duration to 24 h, a reduction of about 13% of PL intensity occurred in nGQD@ZIF-8 dispersion. The visual pictures for the above-described results are presented in Fig. A. 8. The porous structure of ZIF-8 encapsulates the nGQDs, shielding them from exposure to the surrounding environment and reducing the chances of oxidation or other thermal-induced processes that might degrade the nGQDs [52]. These findings demonstrate excellent thermal stability of the synthesized nGQD@ZIF-8 in comparison to bare nGQDs, making them suitable for gas sensing applications that involve high temperature conditions [53]. Gas sensors utilizing nGQD@ZIF-8 would demonstrate robustness in industrial conditions due to their capacity to endure elevated temperatures while maintaining structural integrity as shown in Fig. A. 7b, thereby sustaining dependable performance, and diminishing the requirement for frequent replacements.

Fig. 11 a illustrates the impact of different pH levels on the fluorescent intensity of both nGQDs and nGQD@ZIF-8 solutions, and visual pictures can be referred to in Fig. A. 9. The PL intensity of nGQDs experiences a slight decrease under acidic conditions, while no significant change is observed at pH 7 (neutral). However, when the pH exceeds 7 (alkaline conditions), the PL intensity of nGQDs significantly decreases. This decrease can be attributed to the deprotonation of carboxyl groups, which induces surface charge repulsion and adversely affects the PL emission [44]. In contrast, the PL intensity of nGQD@ZIF-8 exhibits a slight decrease at pH 2 but remains practically constant within a broad pH range (pH 5 to 11). Furthermore, the absence of significant changes in the XRD spectra (Fig. A. 7c) at both pH 2 and pH 11 reveals that the nanocomposite structure remained stable under extreme pH conditions. This observation highlights the considerable potential for utilizing nGQD@ZIF-8 in diverse pH environments, including applications such as drug delivery. For example, by using nGQD@ZIF-8 as carriers, drugs can remain stable and retain their activity in acidic environments commonly found in tumor tissues [54]. The chemically stable and inert nature of the ZIF-8 matrix plays a vital role in preventing nGQDs from interacting with pH-dependent substances that could lead to surface degradation or quenching of PL emission [55].

To assess the PL stability of the nGQDs, and nGQD@ZIF-8, the sample solutions were stored for 3 months. The effect of storage time on the fluorescence intensity was evaluated and depicted in Fig. 11b. From the figure, it is evident that the fluorescence intensity of nGQDs gradually decreases over time during storage. However, the fluorescence intensity nGQD@ZIF-8 remained relatively constant after 3 months of storage, as evidenced by the visual picture in Fig. A. 10. In addition, the nGQD@ZIF-8 was further characterized by TEM and XRD after storage of 3 months and it has been found the morphology of the nanocomposites well maintained (Fig. A. 11) and no obvious crystal structure change occurs in XRD pattern (Fig. A. 7d). These observations indicate that the encapsulation of nGQDs within the ZIF-8 matrix could prevent aggregation of nGQDs, thus preserving the structural integrity and PL properties of the nGQD@ZIF-8 during long-term storage [56]. The remarkable storage stability of nGQD@ZIF-8 makes them highly reliable for practical applications that require extended storage periods [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of PL stabilities of other reported QD@MOF nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 lists the PL stability measurements of nGQD@ZIF-8 compared with other reported QD@MOF nanocomposites. The comparison includes: photostability (the retained PL intensity (%) after certain duration of light exposure); thermal stability (the weight loss (%) or retained PL intensity (%) when heated at a specific temperature; pH stability (changes in PL intensity of the nanocomposites at different pH levels); and storage stability (the retained PL intensity (%) after storage for a particular duration). From the comparison in Tables 1 and it is found that our works demonstrates its prominent superiority, as the stabilities were enhanced in every key aspect crucial for diverse applications. Additionally, based on the reported works in Table 1 [20,21,57–63], the MOFs are believed to serve as protective barriers, preventing degradation and aggregation of QDs in various conditions, as suggested by this study.

In summary, highly blue emissive nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite was successfully developed using a facile bottle-around-ship method at room temperature. The synthesized nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite not only exhibits excellent fluorescence properties of nGQDs mainly due to the doping of nitrogen but also maintains the structural integrity of ZIF-8 after encapsulation. Notably, the nGQD@ZIF-8 nanocomposite demonstrates enhanced PL stability in terms of thermal stability, photostability, pH stability, and storage stability, surpassing that of bare nGQDs. These properties make them highly attractive for various luminescence sensing applications. Even after continous exposure of 12 h UV light, the nGQD@ZIF-8 retains 93.5% of its initial PL intensity. Moreover, in a thermal stability test from 20 °C to 90 °C, the nGQD@ZIF-8 demonstrated strong stability, maintaining 91% of its initial PL intensity even under harsh thermal conditions. In addition, the PL intensity of nGQD@ZIF-8 remains practically constant within a broad pH range (pH 2 to pH 11). Furthermore, not only is the PL intensity of nGQD@ZIF-8 well maintained, but the morphology and structure of the nanocomposites have also been found to remain unchanged after 3 months of storage. These results clearly demonstrate that the encapsulation strategy of nGQDs within ZIF-8 provides effective protection from external factors, eliminating barriers to the application of nGQDs. The improved stabilities of nGQD@ZIF-8 have significant implications for their practical applications, ensuring their long-term viability and functionality.

Thivyah Balakrishnan: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Wei Lun Ang: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Ebrahim Mahmoudi: Methodology, Supervision.
